---
title: "BadranSeq: Publication-Ready Visualisation for Single-Cell RNA Sequencing Data in R"
author:
  - name: Badran Elshenawy
    email: badran.elshenawy@ndm.ox.ac.uk
    corresponding: true
    orcid: 0000-0001-5672-8517
    affiliations:
      - name: Nuffield Department of Medicine, University of Oxford, Oxford, UK
abstract: |
  Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) has transformed our ability to resolve cellular heterogeneity, yet a persistent gap remains between exploratory analysis and publication-quality visualisation. Seurat, the dominant R framework for scRNA-seq, produces plots suited to data exploration but not to direct inclusion in manuscripts. Researchers routinely invest substantial effort in repetitive customisation: annotating PCA axes with variance explained, adding cell borders, styling themes, and constructing split-panel comparisons that preserve spatial context. Here we present BadranSeq, a lightweight R package built on native ggplot2 that closes this gap. BadranSeq provides automatic PCA variance annotation, cell border rendering, silhouette-based split-panel comparisons, statistical violin plots with nonparametric hypothesis testing, Sankey diagrams for categorical metadata, tidy data extraction replacing Seurat's `FetchData()`, interactive cell selection, and a consistent publication theme --- all from single function calls with sensible defaults. With only 19 direct dependencies compared to 56 for SCpubr, BadranSeq achieves a 66\% reduction in dependency footprint while delivering equivalent publication-ready aesthetics. BadranSeq is available under the MIT licence at \url{https://github.com/wolf5996/BadranSeq}.
keywords: [single-cell RNA-seq, visualisation, Seurat, ggplot2, R]
date: today
date-format: "D MMMM YYYY"
bibliography: references.bib
csl: apa.csl
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    papersize: a4
    fontsize: 11pt
    geometry:
      - top=2.5cm
      - bottom=2.5cm
      - left=2.5cm
      - right=2.5cm
    linestretch: 1.15
    indent: true
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: "DarkSlateBlue"
    citecolor: "DarkSlateBlue"
    urlcolor: "DarkSlateBlue"
    number-sections: true
    fig-pos: "htbp"
    keep-tex: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        % ── Fonts ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
        \usepackage{mathpazo}           % Palatino for text and maths
        \usepackage[scaled=0.85]{helvet}% Helvetica for sans-serif
        \usepackage[scaled=0.85]{inconsolata} % Monospace
        \linespread{1.05}               % Palatino needs slightly more leading

        % ── Inline code styling (grey background like GitHub) ────
        \usepackage{soul}
        \definecolor{codebg}{HTML}{F0F0F0}
        \sethlcolor{codebg}
        \let\OldTexttt\texttt
        \renewcommand{\texttt}[1]{\OldTexttt{\hl{\mbox{#1}}}}

        % ── Microtypography ────────────────────────────────────────
        \usepackage{microtype}

        % ── Line numbers (grey, small, non-intrusive) ────────────
        \usepackage{lineno}
        \renewcommand\linenumberfont{\normalfont\tiny\color{gray}}
        \linenumbers

        % ── Section styling ───────────────────────────────────────
        \usepackage{titlesec}
        \titleformat{\section}{\large\bfseries\color{DarkSlateBlue}}{\thesection}{1em}{}
        \titleformat{\subsection}{\normalsize\bfseries\color{DarkSlateBlue}}{\thesubsection}{1em}{}
        \titleformat{\subsubsection}{\normalsize\itshape\color{DarkSlateBlue}}{\thesubsubsection}{1em}{}
        \titlespacing*{\section}{0pt}{1.8ex plus 0.6ex minus 0.2ex}{0.8ex plus 0.2ex}
        \titlespacing*{\subsection}{0pt}{1.4ex plus 0.4ex minus 0.2ex}{0.6ex plus 0.2ex}

        % ── Captions ─────────────────────────────────────────────
        \usepackage[font=small,labelfont=bf,labelsep=period]{caption}

        % ── Headers and footers ──────────────────────────────────
        \usepackage{fancyhdr}
        \pagestyle{fancy}
        \fancyhf{}
        \renewcommand{\headrulewidth}{0.4pt}
        \fancyhead[L]{\small\textit{BadranSeq}}
        \fancyhead[R]{\small\thepage}
        \fancyfoot[C]{\small\textit{Preprint --- not peer reviewed}}

        % ── Tables ───────────────────────────────────────────────
        \usepackage{booktabs}
        \usepackage{array}

        % ── Floats ───────────────────────────────────────────────
        \usepackage{float}
        \usepackage{placeins}
        \setlength{\intextsep}{6pt plus 2pt minus 2pt}      % space above/below in-text floats
        \setlength{\textfloatsep}{8pt plus 2pt minus 4pt}    % space between top/bottom floats and text
        \setlength{\floatsep}{6pt plus 2pt minus 2pt}        % space between consecutive floats
        \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{4pt}                   % space above caption
        \setlength{\belowcaptionskip}{2pt}                   % space below caption
        \renewcommand{\topfraction}{0.9}                     % max fraction of page for top floats
        \renewcommand{\bottomfraction}{0.9}                  % max fraction of page for bottom floats
        \renewcommand{\textfraction}{0.1}                    % min fraction of page for text
        \renewcommand{\floatpagefraction}{0.8}               % min fraction to trigger float page

        % ── Custom title block (Quarto drops affiliations) ──────
        \usepackage{changepage}
        \makeatletter
        \renewcommand{\maketitle}{%
          \begin{center}%
            {\LARGE\bfseries \@title \par}%
            \vskip 1.2em%
            {\large Badran Elshenawy\textsuperscript{1,\,*}\par}%
            \vskip 0.4em%
            {\small%
              \href{https://orcid.org/0000-0001-5672-8517}{ORCID: 0000-0001-5672-8517}%
            \par}%
            \vskip 0.6em%
            {\normalsize\textsuperscript{1}Nuffield Department of Medicine, University of Oxford, Oxford, UK\par}%
            \vskip 0.3em%
            {\small\textsuperscript{*}Corresponding author: \href{mailto:badran.elshenawy@ndm.ox.ac.uk}{badran.elshenawy@ndm.ox.ac.uk}\par}%
            \vskip 0.6em%
            {\normalsize \@date\par}%
          \end{center}%
          \vskip 1.5em%
        }
        \makeatother

        % ── Abstract styling ─────────────────────────────────────
        \renewenvironment{abstract}{%
          \begin{adjustwidth}{1.5cm}{1.5cm}%
          \small%
          \noindent\textbf{Abstract}\par\medskip\noindent\ignorespaces
        }{%
          \end{adjustwidth}\bigskip
        }

        % ── Keywords ─────────────────────────────────────────────
        \providecommand{\keywords}[1]{\par\smallskip\noindent\textbf{Keywords:} #1}
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

## Summary

Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) has become indispensable for dissecting cellular heterogeneity, yet a persistent disconnect separates exploratory analysis from the figures that appear in publications.
The Seurat toolkit [@hao2024; @stuart2019; @butler2018] --- the most widely adopted R framework for scRNA-seq --- provides plotting functions designed for rapid exploration, not for direct inclusion in manuscripts.
As a result, researchers repeatedly rewrite the same polishing steps: formatting PCA variance labels, adding cell borders, applying consistent themes, and building split-panel comparisons that preserve embedding geometry.

BadranSeq addresses this gap with native ggplot2 [@wickham2016] functions that return publication-ready plots with sensible defaults.
Key capabilities include automatic PCA variance annotation, cell border rendering, silhouette-based split comparisons, statistical violin plots via ggstatsplot [@patil2021], Sankey diagrams via ggalluvial [@brunson2020], tidy data extraction, interactive cell selection, and a consistent publication theme.
BadranSeq is available under the MIT licence at <https://github.com/wolf5996/BadranSeq>.

## Statement of Need

scRNA-seq enables gene expression profiling at single-cell resolution and has become a cornerstone of modern biology [@heumos2023; @luecken2019].
Seurat provides comprehensive workflows from raw count matrices through clustering, annotation, and visualisation, making it the *de facto* standard for scRNA-seq analysis in R.
Its plotting functions, however, prioritise flexibility over presentation quality, so the final stages of many projects still involve repeated manual styling.
This repetition is inefficient, encourages inconsistency across analyses, and creates opportunities for avoidable error.

SCpubr [@blancocarmona2022] addressed part of this gap with a broad collection of plotting helpers, but at the cost of a larger dependency footprint: 56 direct dependencies compared to 19 for BadranSeq (@fig-dependency).
That 66\% reduction matters for installation stability, long-term maintenance, and lightweight use cases centred on core dimensionality reduction and feature visualisation.

Additional gaps remain in interactive cell selection, statistical annotation, and tidy data extraction.
Seurat's `CellSelector()` lacks additive and subtractive selection modes, statistical plot annotation often requires a manual workflow, and `FetchData()` returns structures that are awkward for tidy downstream analysis.

BadranSeq provides a lightweight visualisation and data extraction layer covering UMAP, PCA, feature expression, elbow plots, statistical violin plots, Sankey diagrams, and tidy extraction.
Because it builds directly on ggplot2, returned plots remain standard ggplot objects that users can further customise when needed.

## State of the Field

Several tools address scRNA-seq visualisation in R (@fig-comparison, @fig-featureplot, @fig-silhouette, @fig-violin).
Seurat's built-in `DimPlot()` and `FeaturePlot()` [@hao2024] provide practical defaults for exploration but typically require additional styling for publication use.
SCpubr [@blancocarmona2022] offers a broad suite of publication-oriented plots, but requires 56 direct dependencies compared to 19 for BadranSeq (@fig-dependency).
dittoSeq provides colourblind-friendly visualisations with a focus on accessibility.
scater, part of the Bioconductor ecosystem, includes plotting utilities integrated with the SingleCellExperiment class.

BadranSeq occupies a narrower position than SCpubr and a more publication-oriented one than Seurat's native plotting defaults.
It focuses on the core plot types used routinely in Seurat workflows, keeps outputs as standard ggplot objects, and does so with a substantially smaller dependency footprint.
Unlike dittoSeq and scater, it operates directly on Seurat objects, as illustrated across the UMAP, feature, split-panel, and violin comparisons in @fig-comparison, @fig-featureplot, @fig-silhouette, and @fig-violin.

@tbl-comparison summarises the functional differences.

| Feature                        | Seurat | SCpubr | BadranSeq |
|:-------------------------------|:------:|:------:|:---------:|
| Variance explained on PCA axes |   No   |   Yes  |    Yes    |
| Cell borders                   |   No   |   Yes  |    Yes    |
| Cluster labels by default      |   No   |   Yes  |    Yes    |
| Split-panel silhouettes        |   No   |   Yes  |    Yes    |
| Publication theme              |   No   |   Yes  |    Yes    |
| Viridis feature plots          |   No   |   Yes  |    Yes    |
| Interactive cell selection     |  Limited |  No   | Additive / subtractive |
| Statistical violin plots       |   No   |   Yes  | Kruskal--Wallis |
| Sankey / alluvial diagrams     |   No   |   Yes  |    Yes    |
| Tidy data extraction           |  FetchData | No  | Tibble-based |
| Direct dependencies            |   ---  |   56   |    19     |

: Comparison of default capabilities between Seurat, SCpubr, and BadranSeq. {#tbl-comparison}

## Software Design

BadranSeq is organised around a small set of user-facing functions that share a common internal engine and a consistent parameter interface (`object`, `reduction`, `group.by`, `split.by`, `plot_cell_borders`, etc.).
All functions accept a Seurat object as input and return native ggplot2 objects.
This design preserves compatibility with existing Seurat workflows while removing much of the repetitive styling that otherwise occurs at the figure-preparation stage.

The package follows a "sensible defaults" philosophy: functions produce manuscript-ready output without requiring extensive tuning, but still return standard ggplot objects for downstream modification.
`do_DimPlot()` acts as a common entry point and dispatches to reduction-specific helpers such as `do_PcaPlot()` and `do_UmapPlot()`, allowing a consistent interface while preserving specialised behaviour where needed.

### Dimensionality Reduction Visualisations

#### UMAP: Publication-Ready Defaults

UMAP embeddings are often the first figures readers encounter in scRNA-seq studies, so default output quality matters.
Seurat's `DimPlot()` is effective for exploration but usually requires additional work before manuscript use.

BadranSeq's `do_UmapPlot()` applies publication-oriented defaults directly, including cell borders, cluster labels, a tuned categorical palette, and a consistent theme.
This reduces the gap between exploratory and final figures while keeping the output fully editable as a ggplot object.

These changes are not purely cosmetic.
Borders improve readability in dense regions, label placement reduces occlusion, and the colour choices improve visual separation between neighbouring populations.
The contrast between Seurat's default output and BadranSeq's styled result is shown in @fig-comparison.

![Default UMAP comparison with Seurat on top and BadranSeq on bottom. Seurat's `DimPlot()` produces exploratory output requiring manual polishing, while BadranSeq's `do_UmapPlot()` provides publication-ready styling by default.](badranseq_joss_figures/write/figures/fig1_comparison.svg){#fig-comparison width="100%"}

#### PCA: Automatic Variance Annotation

PCA plots are harder to interpret without the variance explained by each component.
Seurat's default PCA visualisation does not include this information, so users often calculate and format it manually before final figure preparation.

BadranSeq's `do_PcaPlot()` adds variance-explained labels automatically for the requested components.
This removes a routine source of manual calculation, keeps axis labelling consistent across PCA figures, and makes interpretation more immediate for readers.

The same variance framework is used in `EnhancedElbowPlot()`, keeping PCA visualisation and variance diagnostics aligned.
This difference is visible in @fig-pca, where the BadranSeq panel exposes the variance context directly on the axes.

![PCA comparison with Seurat on top and BadranSeq on bottom. Seurat's default PCA views omit variance explained, whereas BadranSeq's `do_PcaPlot()` automatically annotates axis labels with variance percentages for immediate interpretability.](badranseq_joss_figures/write/figures/fig2_pca_variance.svg){#fig-pca width="100%"}



### Feature Expression Visualisation

Feature plots are central to marker interpretation, but default colour scaling and point rendering can reduce readability in dense embeddings.
Seurat's `FeaturePlot()` is convenient for exploration, yet publication use often requires manual colour and theme adjustments.

BadranSeq's `do_FeaturePlot()` uses viridis scales [@garnier2024], applies cell borders, and supports quantile-based cutoffs for handling extreme outliers.
These defaults improve comparability across markers and help preserve interpretable dynamic range in multi-panel displays.

Because the output remains a standard ggplot object, users can still refine point size, legends, or other presentation details when needed.
The practical effect of these defaults is shown in @fig-featureplot.

![Feature expression comparison showing Seurat's FeaturePlot() (top row) versus BadranSeq's do_FeaturePlot() (bottom row). Seurat uses default colour scales without cell borders. BadranSeq applies perceptually uniform viridis scales, cell borders for legibility in dense regions, and consistent publication styling. Genes shown: CD3D (T cells), CD8A (cytotoxic T cells), and CD14 (monocytes).](badranseq_joss_figures/write/figures/fig4_featureplot.svg){#fig-featureplot width="100%"}

### Variance Diagnostics

Principal component selection affects clustering and downstream interpretation, yet elbow plots are often presented without explicit variance context.
Seurat's `ElbowPlot()` provides the basic diagnostic, but users still need to translate standard deviations into a more interpretable form.

BadranSeq's `EnhancedElbowPlot()` displays variance explained and can annotate a chosen cutoff directly on the figure.
Together with `get_pca_variance()`, this provides both a clearer visual summary and programmatic access to the same variance calculations used in `do_PcaPlot()`.

The result is a more consistent account of why a given PC range was carried forward into downstream analysis.
This is shown directly in @fig-elbow, where the annotated cutoff is paired with variance-explained scaling.

![Elbow plot comparison. Top: Seurat's default `ElbowPlot()` showing standard deviation by principal component. Bottom: BadranSeq's `EnhancedElbowPlot()` showing variance explained with an annotated cutoff.](badranseq_joss_figures/write/figures/fig5_elbow_colors.svg){#fig-elbow width="80%"}

### Split-Panel Context Preservation

Comparisons across conditions are common in scRNA-seq analysis, but standard faceting can remove the broader embedding context that makes these plots interpretable.
With Seurat's `split.by`, each panel shows only a subset of cells, which can obscure how highlighted populations sit within the full landscape.

BadranSeq addresses this with a silhouette-based approach in `do_UmapPlot()`.
When `split.by` is supplied, all cells remain visible as grey background silhouettes while the cells of interest are overlaid in colour.
This preserves global geometry while still supporting direct condition-level comparison.

The approach is especially useful for treatment, developmental, or longitudinal datasets in which relative positioning within the complete embedding carries biological meaning.
The difference between faceting away context and preserving it with silhouettes is shown in @fig-silhouette.

![Split-panel context preservation comparison. Seurat's default split.by faceting (top row) isolates each group into separate panels, destroying embedding geometry context---cells from other groups are completely absent. BadranSeq's silhouette approach (bottom row) renders all cells as grey backgrounds in each panel, overlaying only target cells in colour, preserving spatial relationships across conditions.](badranseq_joss_figures/write/figures/fig3_split_silhouette.svg){#fig-silhouette width="100%"}

### Statistical Annotation Integration

Comparing expression distributions across cell groups is common, but the standard workflow usually involves separate data extraction, test selection, and plot annotation steps.
That manual process is slow and can introduce inconsistency in both statistical choices and figure formatting.

BadranSeq's `do_StatsViolinPlot()` integrates data extraction, non-parametric testing, multiple-comparison correction, and plot annotation into a single workflow using ggstatsplot [@patil2021].
It performs Kruskal--Wallis testing by default, supports Dunn's pairwise comparisons, and allows focused comparisons through `group.levels`.

This makes statistical violin plots easier to generate reproducibly while keeping the results visually consistent with the rest of the package.
The resulting gain in interpretability is shown in @fig-violin.

![Statistical violin comparison on a matched subset (Naive CD4 T, Memory CD4 T, and CD8 T cells). Left: Seurat's `VlnPlot()` shows expression distributions without statistical annotation. Right: BadranSeq's `do_StatsViolinPlot()` integrates Kruskal--Wallis omnibus testing and Dunn's pairwise comparisons with automatic formatting, enabling immediate interpretation.](badranseq_joss_figures/write/figures/fig6_stats_violin.svg){#fig-violin width="90%"}

### Categorical Relationship Visualisation

Relationships between clusters, annotations, and other metadata are often difficult to inspect using tables alone.
Many-to-many mappings can be especially hard to interpret when categories split or merge across analytical stages.

BadranSeq's `do_SankeyPlot()` uses ggalluvial [@brunson2020] to display these mappings as flows between metadata strata.
This provides a quick visual summary of how strongly categories align, where assignments branch, and which transitions may warrant closer review.

The same approach can be extended beyond cluster annotation to multi-step metadata relationships such as treatment response or developmental progression.
An example linking clusters to annotations is shown in @fig-sankey.

![Sankey diagram showing the relationship between Seurat cluster assignments (left) and cell type annotations (right) in the PBMC 3k dataset.](badranseq_joss_figures/write/figures/fig7_sankey.svg){#fig-sankey width="90%"}

### Dependency Engineering

Dependency footprint matters in research software because installation failures and version conflicts directly affect reproducibility.
This is especially relevant for plotting packages, where useful functionality can be undermined by a large and fragile dependency tree.

BadranSeq adopts a deliberately lightweight approach, with 19 direct dependencies compared to 56 for SCpubr.
That 66\% reduction lowers installation complexity and maintenance burden while still covering the core plot types and utilities that motivate the package.

The comparison in @fig-dependency therefore reflects both software design philosophy and practical usability in long-lived analysis environments.

![Dependency footprint comparison between BadranSeq and SCpubr. BadranSeq maintains 19 direct dependencies compared to 56 for SCpubr, representing a 66\% reduction in dependency burden. This translates to faster installation, fewer version conflicts, and reduced long-term maintenance overhead while achieving equivalent publication-ready aesthetics.](badranseq_joss_figures/write/figures/fig8_dependency_comparison.svg){#fig-dependency width="80%"}

### Data Extraction and Interoperability

Seurat's `FetchData()` is useful, but it often requires additional reshaping before the result can be used comfortably in tidy workflows.
Cell identifiers are not explicit columns, metadata and expression values are mixed together, and expression layer choice can be unclear.

BadranSeq addresses this with two tidy extraction helpers.
`fetch_cell_data()` returns metadata and embeddings with explicit cell identifiers, while `fetch_feature_data()` returns long-format expression data with explicit layer control.
These functions make it easier to move from Seurat objects into dplyr, ggplot2, and custom downstream analyses.

This extraction layer complements the plotting functions by reducing the friction between visualisation, statistics, and further data manipulation.

### Interactive Cell Selection

`select_cells_interactive()` provides a Shiny-based interface for brush-selecting cells from any computed embedding.
Users can additively build a selection across multiple brush operations, subtractively remove cells, and clear to start over.
Selected cells are highlighted with red ring markers in real time, and the function returns either a character vector of barcodes or a subsetted Seurat object.
This is more flexible than Seurat's `CellSelector()`, which lacks additive and subtractive modes.

Additionally, `seurat_sleepwalk()` wraps the sleepwalk package [@ovchinnikova2020] for interactive exploration of how faithfully a 2D embedding preserves high-dimensional distances.

### Performance

For datasets exceeding 50,000 cells, BadranSeq automatically rasterises point layers via ggrastr, preventing prohibitively large vector graphics while keeping text and axes as sharp vector elements.
The threshold is configurable through the `raster` parameter.

## Availability and Reproducibility

BadranSeq is publicly available on GitHub and installable via standard R package managers.
The package ships with a bundled PBMC 3k dataset (SCTransform-normalised, with PCA and UMAP precomputed) for immediate testing and reproducible examples.
A pkgdown documentation site at <https://wolf5996.github.io/BadranSeq/> provides function references and usage vignettes.
Continuous integration via GitHub Actions runs `R CMD check` on macOS, Windows, and Ubuntu.

## Acknowledgements

The aesthetic design of BadranSeq draws on SCpubr [@blancocarmona2022] by Enrique Blanco Carmona.
Design elements adapted from SCpubr include cell border rendering, colour palette generation, and the silhouette split approach.
BadranSeq differs by providing a lighter-weight, native ggplot2 implementation with a 66\% smaller dependency footprint (19 versus 56 direct dependencies) while adding capabilities including PCA variance labels, statistical violin plots, Sankey diagrams, tidy data extraction, and interactive cell selection.

The author thanks the developers of R [@rcore2024], Seurat [@hao2024], ggplot2 [@wickham2016], ggstatsplot [@patil2021], ggalluvial [@brunson2020], and UMAP [@mcinnes2018] for the foundational tools upon which BadranSeq is built.

## References
